coredata.dc:title: An in-vitro study on post-surgical breast wound healing activity by zinc oxide dots and its optimization using Box Behnken design
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology
coredata.dc:creator: Flora, R. Mary Nancy
Palani, S.
Renuka, V.
Chamundeeswari, M.
coredata.dc:description: The development of effective treatments for post-surgical malignant wounds caused by recurrent breast cancer remains challenging. This study aimed to address this issue by creating a biocompatible fluorescent quantum dot using curcumin-loaded Zinc oxide bionanocomposite (Cur-ZnOBC). The ZnOBC was combined with neem essential oil-Aloe-vera gel complex to produce the nanoconjugate Cur-ZnOBC, which was then transformed into curcumin-based quantum dots (Cur-ZnOQDs) using a microwave-assisted method to enhance drug delivery function. The Box-Behnken design was used to optimize the concentration variables involved in the formation of Cur-ZnOQDs. The synthesized Cur-ZnOQDs were found to have low cytotoxicity to human breast cancer cells and exhibited wound healing activity in malignant wounds of breast cancer cells post-surgery. Cell death was noticed only at a concentration of 0.6 mg/mL, and the wound closure area increased from 22.3% to 44.2% at 48 h, which was 20 times greater than the control. Therefore, Cur-ZnOQDs at lower concentration range may act as bio-enhancers to improve outcomes associated with breast cancer and ultimately improve the survival rate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Post-surgical malignant wounds
Breast cancer recurrence
Curcumin-loaded zinc oxide bionanocomposite
Bio-enhancers
Fluorescent quantum dot
Drug delivery function
Neem essential oil-Aloe-vera gel complex

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of ZnOBC from Neem essential oil-Aloe-vera gel complex
Synthesis of curcumin loaded Cur-ZnOBC via transformation into quantum dots (Cur-ZnOQDs)
Curcumin loading capacity and loading efficiency in Cur-ZnOBC nano system
Phase contrast microscopy of MDA-MB231 (Human breast cancer) cells
Data analysis of Box–Behnken design of RSM for Cur-ZnOQDs
Particle size and zeta potential analysis of Cur-ZnOQDs
Optimization and confirmation experiments of Cur-ZnOQDs using RSM
In vitro cytotoxicity assay on MDA-MB231 (Human breast cancer) cells
Wound scratch assay on MDA-MB231 (Human breast cancer) cells
Antimicrobial activity of Cur-ZnOQDs by disk-diffusion method

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Visible absorption spectra of the (a) Cur-ZnOQDs and (b) pure Curcumin.
FT-IR spectrum of the Cur-ZnOQDs, Cur-ZnOBC and pure Curcumin.
XRD analysis of the Cur-ZnOQDs, ZnO NPs and pure Curcumin.
SEM analysis of the (a) Cur-ZnOQDs (b) pure Curcumin and EDX analysis of the (c) Cur-ZnOQDs (d) pure Curcumin.
Particle size analysis of the (a) Cur-ZnOQDs (b) pure Curcumin and Zeta potential of the (c) Cur-ZnOQDs and (d) pure Curcumin.

Contour showing graphics by plotting absorbance versus concentration of (d) A-ZnONps (%) and Curcumin (mg), (e) A-ZnONps (%) and Neem oil (ml), (f) Curcumin (mg) and Neem oil (ml) obtained from Cur-ZnOQDs using BBD of RSM for investigation of interacted parameters belonging to the synthesis of Cur-ZnOQDs.

3D graphics for optimization of response surface by plotting absorbance versus concentration of (a) ZnONps (%) and Curcumin (mg), (b) ZnONps (%) and Neem oil (ml), (c) Curcumin (mg) and Neem oil (ml) obtained from Cur-ZnOQDs using BBD of RSM.

Cell viability and MTT assay images using phase contrast microscopy of MDA-MB231 breast cancer cells treated against Cur-ZnOQDs of concentration ranges: (i) control (ii) 50 μg/ml (iii) 100 μg/ml (iv)200 μg/ml (v) 400 μg/ml (vi) 600 μg/ml respectively.
(b) Graph showing % Cell viability and (c) IC 50 graph of MDA-MB231 breast cancer cells treated against Cur-ZnOQDs.

(a): Wound closure images using phase contrast microscopy of MDA-MB231 breast cancer cells treated against Cur-ZnOQDs: Control -(i) 0th h (ii) 24th h (iii) 48th h, Cur-ZnOQDs- (iv) 0th h (v) 24th h (vi) 48th h. (b) Graph showing %wound closure of control and Cur-ZnOQDs.
Inhibition zones of the Cur-ZnOQDs against (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus.

head.ce:title: An in-vitro study on post-surgical breast wound healing activity by zinc oxide dots and its optimization using Box Behnken design

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Arunai Engineering College, Tiruvannamalai, 606603, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Assistant professor, Department of Chemical Engineering, Arunai Engineering College, Tiruvannamalai-606603, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, Arunai Engineering College, Tiruvannamalai, 606603, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, St. Joseph's College of Engineering, Chennai, 600119, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Professor, Department of Chemical Engineering, St. Joseph’s College of Engineering, Chennai- 600119, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, St. Joseph's College of Engineering, Chennai, 600119, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Associate professor, Department of Biotechnology, St. Joseph’s College of Engineering, Chennai- 600119, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of effective treatments for post-surgical malignant wounds caused by recurrent breast cancer remains challenging. This study aimed to address this issue by creating a biocompatible fluorescent quantum dot using curcumin-loaded Zinc oxide bionanocomposite (Cur-ZnOBC). The ZnOBC was combined with neem essential oil-Aloe-vera gel complex to produce the nanoconjugate Cur-ZnOBC, which was then transformed into curcumin-based quantum dots (Cur-ZnOQDs) using a microwave-assisted method to enhance drug delivery function. The Box-Behnken design was used to optimize the concentration variables involved in the formation of Cur-ZnOQDs. The synthesized Cur-ZnOQDs were found to have low cytotoxicity to human breast cancer cells and exhibited wound healing activity in malignant wounds of breast cancer cells post-surgery. Cell death was noticed only at a concentration of 0.6 mg/mL, and the wound closure area increased from 22.3% to 44.2% at 48 h, which was 20 times greater than the control. Therefore, Cur-ZnOQDs at lower concentration range may act as bio-enhancers to improve outcomes associated with breast cancer and ultimately improve the survival rate.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer is the most frequent cancer in women, and the number of cases is increasing each year. Unfortunately, the annual global incidence of breast cancer cases remains over one million, indicating the danger of breast cancer to women's health [1]. Women who undergo breast cancer surgery face a series of problems with complex breast wounds. If not treated, breast cancer can spread to other parts of the body, such as the liver, bone, brain, lung, and skin [2]. Skin flap necrosis, epidermolysis, and surgical wound infection are all common and serious complications of breast cancer surgery [3]. According to psychological research, a perceived or real method of control treatment throughout post-surgical breast cancer treatment assists the patient in escaping uncomfortable situations or senses such as anxiety and pain [4]. Wound healing following a surgical procedure is a challenging and complicated process [5,6]. Breast cancer wounds, if left untreated, can cause severe pain, large-scale wound infection, and even death due to fluid loss in patients. It is critical to protect wounds from tissue damage as soon as possible and to avoid fluid loss and bacterial infection in the wound area [7–9]. Wound healing is characterized by complex cellular, biochemical, and physiological activities that are influenced by a variety of local, systemic, and biological conditions such as wound humidity, oxygenation, infection, and maceration, as well as age, nutritional status, obesity, medications, and smoking habits [10–12]. After healing, the connective tissue of the wound site should be repaired and re-epithelialized, and it should be restored to its normal anatomical properties and structural integrity [13]. Chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and drugs with severe side effects are among the cancer treatment options available. These treatments are excruciatingly painful, causing strain and deteriorating the patient's health [14]. Clinicians have already primarily focused on discovering novel potential therapeutic agents to promote faster skin repair, especially those made from natural herbal extracts, due to their ease of availability and non-toxicity, as chemically synthesized drugs have a plethora of side effects. A literature review on the wound healing activity of Neem leaf extract, Aloe vera gel, Curcumin, and Neem essential oil was conducted, and the presence of active phytochemicals such as flavonoids, terpenoids, coumarins, alkaloids, tannins, sulphurous compounds, carbohydrates, proteins, and minerals was discovered. These natural components have antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anti-diabetic, anticancer, antiviral, antibacterial, antigingivitic, antifungal, antiulcer, hepatoprotective, neuroprotective, antipyretic, and wound healing properties [15].

As a consequence, an advanced agent must have the greatest possible efficiency in wound closure, as well as the greatest possible cytotoxicity [16,17]. Nowadays, there is a persistent demand for the design and development of new biodegradable and biocompatible components for wound management that stimulate and enhance the process of healing damaged cells and preserve their integrity [12]. A wound healing agent should be intended to stimulate tissue repair, remove abscesses, and prevent infection at the wound site [18]. Furthermore, local administration of antibiotics at therapeutic levels can be beneficial in reducing bacterial growth in the wound, preventing infection diffusion, and avoiding systemic toxicity via other administration routes, such as oral delivery [13].

Nanotechnology enhances wound healing with nanostructures like Bionanocomposites (BCs) and diverse materials such as crystals, quantum dots (QDs), graphene sheets, and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [8,19–21]. Inorganic nanoparticles like zinc oxide, iron oxide, titanium oxide, and silver nanoparticles are popular for their versatile applications in drug delivery, photoluminescence, textiles, photocatalysis, wound healing, and antibacterial agents [6,22–24]. QDs have garnered attention among nanomaterials for their versatile surface chemistry, small size, and luminescent and electromagnetic properties. They enable monitoring of QDs' automotive transport and drug delivery at both the structural and cellular levels [25–30]. Recently, “Green synthesis” methods, known for their environmental friendliness and low cost, have gained interest for synthesizing QDs [31].

The development of new pharmaceuticals containing active substances using trial-and-error methodology is time-consuming, costly, and laborious. The Response Surface Methodology (RSM) is often used to determine the primary impact, interaction, quadratic effects, and response surface shape. The Box Behnken design (BBD) is an independent and objective, hinged quadratic design with no built-in factorial or fractional factorial points and partial correlations at the midpoints of variable space's edges as well as at the centre [32].

Curcumin, a phenolic compound found in turmeric rhizomes (Curcuma longa Linn), is widely used in the pharmaceutical industry due to its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties [33–36]. However, its poor solubility in water means that either no curcumin or very little curcumin is detected in plasma or cells after administration [37]. Chandrasekaran Karthikeyan et al. prepared chitosan-curcumin ZnO-based nanoparticles for antimicrobial activity [38]. Yong Zhao et al. demonstrated the use of curcumin-loaded nanocomposites in wound healing applications [39]. Firdos Alam Khan et al. synthesized quantum dots encapsulated with curcumin and performed studies on their breast cancer activity [40]. Niyaz Ahmad et al. generated curcumin Nano emulsions for wound healing applications [41].

The motivating wound healing activity of curcumin, as found in the literature survey, drove our objective of developing a new method for preparing curcumin-based quantum dots (Cur-ZnOQDs) for wound healing applications. We utilized an environmentally friendly bionanocomposite (ZnOBC) loaded with curcumin (Cur-ZnOBC) and microwave-assisted techniques to form fluorescent Cur-ZnOQDs, suitable for drug delivery due to their small size. The research focused on exploring the wound healing activity of Cur-ZnOQDs, an area with limited previous research. Optimization using the Box Behnken Design allowed efficient process variable optimization. Our sustainable materials and innovative synthesis method offer promising potential for producing medical-grade Cur-ZnOQDs. This study establishes a foundation for investigating Cur-ZnOQDs as a bio-enhancer in breast cancer therapy, utilizing their visualization capabilities for drug delivery assessment. The optimized Cur-ZnOQDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility and wound healing activity. The growing popularity of quantum dots in wound dressings highlights their significance in nano-biomedicine. This study contributes valuable insights and paves the way for innovative and cost-effective cancer treatment using natural ingredients, without relying on expensive equipment or harsh chemicals.

This study has potential implications in the development of therapeutic regimens for post-surgical malignant wounds caused by recurrent breast cancer. The in-vitro analysis of Cur-ZnOQDs did not cause significant harm or damage to human breast cancer cells. This is a promising result because if a treatment is toxic to cancer cells, it could also be toxic to healthy cells, leading to unwanted side effects in patients. Therefore, the fact that the Cur-ZnOQDs showed low toxicity to human breast cancer cells suggests that the treatment has the potential to be well-tolerated by patients. However, the low cytotoxicity of the Cur-ZnOQDs at less concentration range is certainly a promising result that can support the further investigation through in-vivo analysis. Moreover, the in-vitro wound healing activity of the Cur-ZnOQDs in malignant wounds of breast cancer cells post-surgery is a significant finding. This suggests that the treatment could help improve the healing process and potentially reduce the risk of wound complications following surgery. Curcumin, Neem essential oil and Aloe-vera gel have been used for centuries in traditional medicine for their anti-inflammatory and wound healing properties [31,42]. The use of these natural products in the production of Cur-ZnOQDs could make the treatment more accessible to patients and potentially reduce the cost of treatment. Also, these green synthesized quantum dots in future can be administered in topical application approach.

Pharmaceutical grade Zinc Sulphate (ZnSO4) was purchased from Hi tech enterprise Pvt Ltd, Chennai and Curcumin was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Aloe vera gel and Neem leaves obtained from nearby local farms at Tiruvannamalai district and Neem essential oil was purchased from nearby organic shop at Tiruvannamalai, Tamil Nadu.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnOBC from Neem essential oil-Aloe-vera gel complex
ce:section.ce:para: About 30 g of neem leaves were thoroughly washed in distilled water, dried, cut into fine pieces, and crushed. 1 L of distilled water was added and boiled for about 15 min and filtered with Whatman No.1 filter paper (pore size 35 μm) to yield the extract. For the synthesis of ZnO nanoparticles, a 1 mM aqueous solution of zinc sulphate (ZnSO4) and an aqueous leaf extract of Azadirachta indica were used (ZnONps). Drop by drop, 20 ml of Azadirachta indica leaf extract was added to 80 ml of 1 mM ZnSO4 aqueous solution. It was stirred in a magnetic stirrer for 24 h at 37 °C [42]. For 2–3 h 0.5% ZnONps was dispersed in 1 g of Aloe vera gel and sonicated for 5 min at 50 °C. After that, 0.5 ml Tween 80 and 0.5 ml Neem essential oil were added drop by drop and stirred for 24 h to achieve a homogeneous mixture [43]. Finally, the precipitate was dried in an oven at 50 °C overnight. As a result, a black powder known as neem essential oil-Aloe-vera gel complex derived zinc oxide bionanocomposite (ZnOBC) was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of curcumin loaded Cur-ZnOBC via transformation into quantum dots (Cur-ZnOQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin (5 mg/mL) solution was added drop wise in the same ratio as ZnOBC suspension in water. The colour changed from yellow to orange after stirring. The fine orange nanomaterials were obtained by centrifugation of the suspension, and the resulting nanomaterial (Cur-ZnOBC) was dried in an oven at 40 °C [22]. Using 500 mg of Cur-ZnOBC suspended in 30 ml of distilled water and magnetic stirring for 15 min, 20 ml of 50 mg/ml Cur-ZnOQDs were prepared. The Cur-ZnOBC suspension was then microwaved for 15 min before being centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to aid in the synthesis of Cur-ZnOQDs [43]. The supernatant was collected, examined under UV light Trans-illuminator, and stored for future use. The formation of Cur-ZnOQDs is depicted in Scheme 1 .

The prepared Cur-ZnOQDs were analyzed through various measurement systems. Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus spectrophotometer was used to measure the UV–Vis absorption of Cur-ZnOQDs in the 200–600 nm range. The characteristic functional groups of Cur-ZnOQDs were determined using Fourier Transform InfraRed (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The samples were prepared in KBr pellets, and the results were obtained in the infrared range 400–4000 cm−1. X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was used to study the phase and crystallinity of Cur-ZnOBC, and powdered samples were performed on a Rigaku diffractometer in the 5°–80° range. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were taken by a fluorescence spectrometer (LS, PerkinElmer). SEM micrographs were taken by analyzing the prepared grids on Philips CM200 SEM operating at 200 kV (100 nm resolution) instrument. EDX (energy dispersive X-ray) analysis was done to identify the elements present in Cur-ZnOQDs. Using a particle size analyzer, the particle size of Cur-ZnOQDs was determined further (Microtrac/Singapore Model Nanotrac wave). Zeta potential of synthesized Cur-ZnOQDs was analyzed to determine the charges present on the surface of Cur-ZnOQDs. TEM imaging was carried out using a JEOL JEM-2100F operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The sample was inserted into the TEM column and images were acquired using a Gatan CCD camera to analyze the size and morphology of Cur-ZnOQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Curcumin loading capacity and loading efficiency in Cur-ZnOBC nano system
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the curcumin loading capacity, we need to know the amount of Cur-ZnOBC used. For which 500 mg of Cur-ZnOBC was suspended in 30 mL of distilled water. Assuming that the entire curcumin content in the Cur-ZnOBC suspension is successfully loaded, the curcumin loading capacity was found to be 2.5 mg. This was calculated by using Equation 1. To calculate the loading efficiency, we need to compare the amount of curcumin loaded to the initial amount of curcumin used. For which 20 mL of a 50 mg/mL Cur-ZnOQDs solution was prepared. Assuming that all the curcumin in the Cur-ZnOBC suspension was successfully converted into Cur-ZnOQDs, the loading efficiency of 400% has been obtained using Equation 2.
Curcumin Loading Capacity = (Amount of Cur-ZnOBC used) x (Curcumin concentration in Cur-ZnOBC suspension) → (1)
Curcumin Loading Efficiency = (Amount of curcumin loaded/Initial amount of curcumin used) x 100%→ (2)

In this experiment, the human breast cancer cell line MDA-MB231 was seeded in 96-well plates with a concentration of 1 × 104 cells/well in DMEM media supplemented with 1× Antibiotic Antimycotic Solution and 10% fetal bovine serum. The cells were then treated with different concentrations of a test compound in serum-free media and incubated for 24 h. After treatment, the medium was removed, and the cells were washed with 1× PBS. Next, 0.5 mg/mL MTT in 1× PBS was added to each well, and the plate was incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 4 h. The MTT-containing medium was then removed, and the cells were washed with PBS (200 μl). The formed formazan dye crystals were dissolved in 100 μl of DMSO and mixed thoroughly [44,45]. The intensity of the purple-blue formazan dye was evaluated at 570 nm using a microplate reader.

The MDA-MB231 cells used in this study were obtained from NCCS, Pune, India, and were seeded onto 12-well plates (Tarsons, India) at a final cell density of 2X105 cells/well for the scratch assay. The cells were cultured in high glucose Dulbecco's Modified Essential Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (Himedia, India) and 1× Antibiotic Antimycotic Solution (Himedia, India) in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. The cells were allowed to adhere overnight at 37 °C and 5% CO2 before performing the scratch assay. A sterile 200 μl tip was used to scratch the cell monolayers, and the wells were treated with a test compound at a concentration of 100 μg/ml. The cells were then incubated in a 37 °C and 5% CO2 incubator, and images of the wound area were captured at different time points (0, 24, and 48 h). The percentage of wound closure was analyzed using the imageJ tool [46–48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase contrast microscopy of MDA-MB231 (Human breast cancer) cells
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of Cur-ZnOQDs on MDA-MB231 cells was assessed by examining changes in cell morphology and chromatin condensation. The MDA-MB231 cells were stained with propidium iodide and observed under a phase contrast microscope.

All tests were performed in triplicate and the results were expressed at the means ± standard error. Data analysis consisted of one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Friedman test showed significance at p < 0.05 for wound healing activity of Cur-ZnOQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Data analysis of Box–Behnken design of RSM for Cur-ZnOQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorbance response of Cur-ZnOQDs was estimated by the Box–Behnken design (BBD) of Response surface methodology (RSM). Design Expert-13 software was applied to analyze the variables of response involved in the experimental formation of Cur-ZnOQDs [49]. The factors with concentration of ZnONps (X1), Curcumin (X2), and Neem oil (X3) were used to prepare the design which provided the absorbance response as final result. About 17 runs were possible in this work for the chosen factors. Levels and range of variables were monitored. To evaluate the statistical experimental analysis all the experiments were done three times. Overall, Photoluminescence was carried using derived optimum values after the formation of Cur-ZnOQDs to determine the importance of BBD.

The antimicrobial activities of Cur-ZnOQDs were evaluated using the disc-diffusion method (Standards Institute Clinical Laboratory (CLSI) 2006; Pobiega et al., 2019) against two bacterial strains, namely Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. The bacterial strains were first cultured on Nutrient Agar (Himedia, India) at 37 °C for 24 h. Bacterial inocula were then prepared in a sterile saline (0.85% NaCl) (w/v) solution, with a quantity corresponding to 0.5 McFarland (∼1 × 108 cfu/mL). The test pathogens were spread on Mueller-Hinton agar (MHA) plates, and discs of diameter 6 mm containing 100 μg of Cur-ZnOQDs were placed over the agar plate. Ciprofloxacin (20 μg/disc) was used as a standard antibacterial agent, respectively. The test plates were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C, and the zone of inhibition (mm in diameter) was measured and recorded as the activity against the test pathogen [50].

The UV–Visible analysis of the Cur-ZnOQDs and pure Curcumin was analyzed by the UV–Visible spectrometer. Cur-ZnOQDs exhibited the major peak at 203 nm [51]. However, the pure Curcumin showed peak shift at 259 nm. The Cur-ZnOQDs (Fig. 1 a) showed high peak which confirms the presence of ZnO but pure Curcumin (Fig. 1b) failed to exhibit peak which is due to the absence of ZnO. These results confirmed that ZnO is blended with natural components like Curcumin in Cur-ZnOQDs. These results further confirmed the FT-IR results.

The Curcumin loading capacity and Curcumin loading efficiency of Curcumin in Cur-ZnOQDs were also determined using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The process involved diluting 100 mg of Cur-ZnOBC in 5 ml of ethanol solution, followed by magnetic stirring for 10 min. The resulting supernatant was collected and its optical density (OD) was measured at a wavelength of 431 nm. The obtained OD value was recorded as 0.6, which matched results from previous research [52–54]. From the OD values obtained for various concentration of Curcumin, the Curcumin concentration is the loading capacity of Curcumin on Cur-ZnOQDs and was found to be as 4.6 mg/ml using a calibration curve (Supplementary Fig. 3). The Curcumin loading efficiency was also calculated using (supplementary Equation.1) and the Curcumin loading efficiency was obtained as 92%. This calculation takes into account the initial amount of curcumin used and the amount detected in the supernatant, providing insights into the effectiveness of the loading process of Curcumin on Cur-ZnOQDs.

FT-IR analysis was done for Cur-ZnOQDs, Cur-ZnOBC and pure Curcumin (Fig. 2 ). Peaks of 605 cm−1 (Cur-ZnOQDs) and 615 cm−1 (Cur-ZnOBC) indicates the ZnO stretching and also blending of ZnO in quantum dots and composite with other natural components to form Cur-ZnOQDs and Cur-ZnOBC. The band at 505 cm−1 (Cur-ZnOQDs), 464 cm−1 (Cur-ZnOBC) and 813 cm−1 (pure Curcumin) indicates the C–C bond. The peak at 1112 cm−1 (Cur-ZnOQDs), 1097 cm−1 (Cur-ZnOBC) and 1282 cm−1 (pure Curcumin) is due to the C–O bond. Other peaks at 1471 cm−1 (Cur-ZnOQDs), 1481 cm−1 (Cur-ZnOBC) and 1512 cm−1 (pure Curcumin) are due to CC bond. The sharp peak at 2359 cm−1 is because of CO bond present in pure Curcumin. The broad bands at 3508 cm−1 (Cur-ZnOQDs), 3442 cm−1 (Cur-ZnOBC) and 3433 cm−1 (pure Curcumin) are due to the presence of OH stretching [55]. All these bands formation is possible due to the presence of carbon, oxygen and hydrogen present in the photochemical of natural components mainly Curcumin. As a result, Curcumin helps in the water solubility ability of Cur-ZnOQDs which helps in fast drug delivery. The C N bond is formed by the peak at 1625 cm−1 of Cur-ZnOQDs and 1627 cm−1 of Cur-ZnOBC is due to the presence of amines blending in natural components like Neem essential oil, A. Vera gel and Neem leaf extract. The shared peaks in the FTIR spectra of Cur-ZnOQDs and Cur-ZnOBC provide evidence of common chemical characteristics or interactions. This similarity suggests that certain molecular components or structural elements present in the composite material are also present in the QDs.

Fig. 3 depicts the XRD pattern and lattice points of Cur-ZnOQDs, ZnO NPs and pure Curcumin. As per the reference data present in JCPDS no. 36–1451 for ZnO wurtzite structure, the diffraction peaks are well indexed with lattice points for hexagonal ZnO wurtzite structure of Cur-ZnOQDs, ZnO NPs and pure Curcumin [56]. Highly intense peaks were seen at ZnO lattices of Cur-ZnOQDs when compared with that of ZnO NPs and pure Curcumin. Phytochemicals present in natural components of neem leaf extract, neem essential oil, A. Vera gel and Curcumin are responsible for hydrogen bonds by carbohydrates as shown by intense peaks of XRD pattern of Cur-ZnOQDs. Also, high intense peaks values at lattice points 101 (20.1°), 101 (19.9°) and 102 (21.7°) for Cur-ZnOQDs, ZnO NPs and pure Curcumin respectively are used to calculate the crystalline size of Cur-ZnOQDs, ZnO NPs and pure Curcumin. The crystalline size was found to be 10.8 nm for Cur-ZnOQDs, 37.1 nm for ZnO NPs and 104.3 nm for pure Curcumin using the Debye–Scherrer relation shown in Eq. 3 [57].
(3) D = Kλ/βCosθ→
Where K = 0.89 (a constant), λ (0.15406 nm) is the X-ray wavelength, β denotes the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the selected XRD peak and θ is the Bragg's angle. Hence, from the above observations the low Crystalline size of Cur-ZnOQDs was obtained due to the natural precursors present in Cur-ZnOQDs.

Photoluminescence spectrum of the Cur-ZnOQDs shown in Fig. 4 . The peak at 532 nm was obtained by excitation frequency of 450 nm. This is further confirmed by the visual observation of strong fluorescent property of Cur-ZnOQDs which shows a bluish green colour glow when seen with naked eye when the quantum dots were kept under UV-transilluminator (inset Fig. 4a). Emission frequency of Cur-ZnOQDs shows a strong intense peak at 377 nm at a frequency of 365 nm this confirms the presence of ZnO in Cur-ZnOQDs which helps in providing fluorescence property to Cur-ZnOQDs [56].

Surface morphology of synthesized Cur-ZnOBC was observed by SEM which observed that particles are hexagonal shape. Micrograph of synthesized Cur-ZnOBC was shown in Fig. 5 a seen that average particle size of Cur-ZnOBC particles is below 36 nm. EDX analysis showed the elemental composition of Cur-ZnOBC (Fig. 5b). This analysis confirmed the oxygen and zinc signal in the Cur-ZnOBC prepared by green route methods [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size and zeta potential analysis of Cur-ZnOQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Particle size distribution and Zeta potential (Zetasizer NanoZS, Malvern Instruments, UK) were used to measure and compare the stability of Cur-ZnOQDs and pure Curcumin. The measurements were obtained by averaging three repeated measurements. The particle size distributions of Cur-ZnOQDs and pure Curcumin were generally narrow, with mean particle sizes of 10.92 nm and 100.87 nm respectively, as shown in Fig. (6a & b) . This demonstrates that the obtained Cur-ZnOQDs are smaller in size than pure Curcumin. Surface charges of about −2.7 mV, and −9.1 mV were observed for the Cur-ZnOQDs and pure Curcumin respectively, indicating the presence of Cur-ZnOQDs formation and blending of Zinc sulphate, A. vera, Curcumin and Neem leaf extract in Cur-ZnOBC. This had an effect on the size and surface charges of Cur-ZnOQDs. The stability of quantum dots was suggested, and the value of zeta potential for Cur-ZnOQDs is within the stable low range when compared to pure Curcumin [59]. As a result of the residual surfactants, Cur-ZnOQDs may accumulate and act as more stable components.

The size and morphology of Cur-ZnOQDs were studied through TEM analysis, which showed that the particles had a uniform size distribution of 12 nm and a well-formed structure (Fig. 7 ). The TEM images revealed that the Cur-ZnOQDs were well-dispersed and spherical in shape [60]. The successful blending of Zinc sulphate with natural agents present in Cur-ZnOQDs contributed to the formation of the uniform-sized nanoparticles. Additionally, the particle size results obtained from the TEM analysis were supported by those from the particle size analyzer, further confirming the particle size distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization and confirmation experiments of Cur-ZnOQDs using RSM
ce:section.ce:para:
Box–Behnken design (BBD) of experiments was applied for the optimization of variables concentration for the formation of Cur-ZnOQDs. Demand in research for product development with desired characteristics with accurate estimate, low cost and high quality of products was done by using BBD. Analysis of variance (ANOVA), regression equation and regression coefficients were analyzed for absorbance data of Cur-ZnOQDs. The polynomial equation shown below in equation (4) represents the functional groups of the absorbance data of the concentration factors of A-ZnONps, Curcumin, and Neem oil.
(4)
Y =
β 0
∑
i = 1
3
β i
x i
+
∑
i = 1
3
β i
i
x i 2
+
∑
i = 1
3
∑
j = i + 1
3
β i
j
x i
x j
→
R2 = 0.9117 was obtained from the ANOVA analysis which indicated that the coefficient of determination for the model is significant. The interaction between the chosen factors was suitable indicating that model obtained a F-value as 10.68. The formation of Cur-ZnOQDs showed larger effect on absorbance value by showing p < 0.05 for the independent factors A, B, and C also for quadratic terms like AB, AC, BC and A2, B2, C2. Fig. 8 shows the 2D contour plots and Fig. 9 shows the 3D response surface plots obtained by the reactions of Cur-ZnOQDs absorbance using Eq. (1). To explain these factors responses, one variable was kept constant and other two factors were varied and shapes of response surfaces and contour plots were obtained by these factors' interaction [61]. The elliptical nature of the contour plots depicts that the interactions with natural components present in Cur-ZnOQDs were perfectly blended (Fig. 8). Response surface plots depicted the absorbance nature of Cur-ZnOQDs at 530 nm. For these results, the optimum parameters were fixed as concentration ranges of A = 0.5 % (ZnONPs), B = 5 mg (Curcumin), and C = 0.5 ml (Neem oil). Under these optimum conditions all the tests were conducted thrice to obtain the accurate absorbance value of Cur-ZnOQDs. These absorbance values of Cur-ZnOQDs matched the results conducted by the photoluminescence spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity assay on MDA-MB231 (Human breast cancer) cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of Cur-ZnOQDs was evaluated by MTT assay MDA-MB231 breast cancer cells. Untreated cells were used as control for the comparison of treated cells. The control (untreated cells) shows 100% cell viability which is used to show variation of cytotoxicity when treated with Cur-ZnOQDs. The breast cancer cell line was treated against 5 mg/ml of Cur-ZnOQDs of concentrations ranging from 50 μg/mL to 600 μg/mL and showed % cell viability of 77.59 %–5.72 % respectively for these concentration range. These are supported by the phase contrast microscopy images as shown in Fig. 10 a. Therefore, as the dose of Cur-ZnOQDs increases the cell viability also increased as shown in Fig. 10b. In our study, we observed an increase in cell viability with an increase in the dose of Cur-ZnOQDs (Fig. 10 b). However, it is important to note that Cur-ZnOQDs are highly toxic at higher doses. Therefore, for wound healing purposes, a lower dose of Cur-ZnOQDs should be selected to prevent potential high toxicity during the wound healing process. To know the efficacy of Cur-ZnOQDs IC50 value for the quantum dots treated cells was calculated and the results was obtained as IC50 = 140.1 ± 0.17 μg/ml (Fig. 10c). All the experiments were carried thrice for accuracy as given in supplementary file (Table s1 and 2). This low concentration range indicates how much drug is needed to inhibit a biological process by half, thus providing a measure of potency of an antagonist drug in pharmacological research. Hence, based on IC50 data, a low concentration range of 100 μg/mL of Cur-ZnOQDs was fixed for performing wound healing activity of MDA-MB231 breast cancer cells.

To compare the results, we also treated the normal mouse fibroblast cell line (L929) with Cur-ZnOQDs using the same concentration range as in the MDA-MB231 cell treatment. In contrast to the breast cancer cells, the L929 fibroblast cell line showed constant cell viability of around 99.49% as the dose of Cur-ZnOQDs increased. The cell viability at this dose level was similar to the negative control, which was at 100%. These results were supported by phase contrast microscopy images (Figs. 1 and 2 (supplementary)), confirming that Cur-ZnOQDs did not significantly affect the cell viability of L929 cells, as the viability remained close to the control level. Overall, the findings suggest that Cur-ZnOQDs may have different effects on different cell types. They have a positive effect on the viability of MDA-MB231 breast cancer cells, potentially promoting cell growth or reducing cell death, while showing minimal effects on the viability of L929 fibroblast cells. Additionally, the drug efficacy of Cur-ZnOQDs can be attributed to their reduced particle size and rich carbon content, mainly due to the presence of Curcumin, a known compound with wound healing properties. Also, these results emphasize the importance of carefully selecting the appropriate dosage of Cur-ZnOQDs for specific applications to harness their beneficial effects while minimizing potential toxicity concerns, especially in wound healing and cancer-related research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wound scratch assay on MDA-MB231 (Human breast cancer) cells
ce:section.ce:para: The wound healing activity of Cur-ZnOQDs at a concentration range of 100 μg/mL was assessed based on the MTT assay performed with MDA-MB231 breast cancer cells. Wound scratch assay was performed using untreated cells (control) in comparison with treated cells against Cur-ZnOQDs. The wound closure area was observed to be 72.5 % at 24 h and 81.9 % at 48 h for control. When the MDA-MB231 breast cancer cells were treated with Cur-ZnOQDs (100 μg/mL) showed a wound closure area of 22.3 % at 24hr and 41.2 % at 48 h. From the above observations Cur-ZnOQDs had 20 times greater cell migration or wound healing activity and control (untreated cells) had cell migration only 10 times (Fig. 11 a). Wound closure data are also given in supplementary file (Table3). These data were supported by graph of % wound closure vs. Control and Cur-ZnOQDs (Fig. 11b). Therefore, the fluorescence property and reduced particle size of Cur-ZnOQDs are responsible for wound healing activity faster. Photochemicals present in natural agents like Curcumin, A. Vera gel, neem oil and neem leaf extract blended in Cur-ZnOQDs are also the main reason for the wound healing activity against MDA-MB231 breast cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial activity of Cur-ZnOQDs by disk-diffusion method
ce:section.ce:para: After evaluating the wound healing activity of a compound, it is important to also assess its antimicrobial activity to ensure that the healing effect is not compromised by any potential infection. In this study, the antimicrobial activity of Cur-ZnOQDs was evaluated using the disk diffusion method against two bacterial strains, Escherichia coli (a gram-negative bacteria) and Staphylococcus aureus (a gram-positive bacteria) at various concentration range of 25, 50, 75 and 100 μg/disc [62,63]. The diameter of the zone of inhibition for Cur-ZnOQDs has obtained at 100 μg/disc against E. coli was 20 mm, while the diameter of the zone of inhibition against S. aureus was 8 mm. These results suggest that Cur-ZnOQDs have stronger antimicrobial activity against E. coli than against S. aureus, as shown in Fig. 12 (a & b). Also, this study found that Cur-ZnOQDs exhibited wound healing activity at a low concentration range of 100 μg/mL, and also demonstrated antimicrobial activity at a low concentration range. Therefore, it is crucial to investigate the antimicrobial activity of the compound alongside its wound healing activity to ensure safe and effective clinical applications.

Curcumin loaded Zinc oxide quantum dots were formed from Curcumin Bionanocomposite with reduced particle size of 10 nm. This enhanced the surface penetration property of Quantum dots which helped in the easy wound healing activity against MDA-MB231 breast cancer cells. Cur-ZnOQDs showed 20 times higher wound closure area when compared to untreated cells. Also, Cur-ZnO QDs have been reported to exhibit good antimicrobial activity and this property can help prevent infections in wounds, thereby potentially aiding in the wound healing process. The natural components like Curcumin present in the Quantum dots helped in the wound healing activity. The synthesize of novel quantum dots can help in the wound infection after breast cancer surgery. From this study, it is believed that Cur-ZnOQDs is highly promising nanomaterial, which will be suitable for rehabilitation of wounds during breast cancer surgeries, along with mastectomy, breast conserving surgery, breast reconstruction, and auxillary lymph node dissection (ALND) due to the increase in breast cancers in young women because of bad food habits, genetics, stress, health care avoidance, variety of lifestyle and environmental conditions. This in turn can help the surgeons or researchers in the field of breast cancer surgery to change the lifestyle of women facing issues with post-surgical breast cancer wounds. In addition, the Box-Behnken design proved to be a highly effective tool for optimizing the concentration variables involved in the formation of Cur-ZnOQDs. The results obtained through this method were further validated by the close match between the absorbance values of Cur-ZnOQDs and the photoluminescence spectra. Hence, Cur-ZnOQDs may be regarded as potential drug delivery nano carrier for wound care management.
The author declares that there is no ethical issues in this research work.
Mary Nancy Flora R - Methodology, Formal analysis and investigation, Writing - original draft preparation.
Palani S - Resources, Supervision.
Renuka V - Statistical Analysis.
Chamundeeswari M - Conceptualization, Writing - review and editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jddst.2023.105094.
